item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I do not recognize any bodies in the water, that landscape seems oddly familiar. Should I be worrkijiogömkv hmi,k.—hi

item.title: Re: I Expected Something Else In The Water
item.content:encoded: Can't please everyone.

item.title: I Expected Something Else In The Water
item.content:encoded:
I've heard a LOT of references to the phrase "you don't recognize the bodies in the water" and…

I was just suddenly let down. This is the iconic phrase?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How many times has the ACS header been changed within the last few months?

item.title: location
item.content:encoded:
So are the number of letters in the lake's name and county meaningful?

+ show block
– hide block

If so it looks like Culver must be a Foundation town.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was the ACS header at the top of the article changed within the last month?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Funny "bodies in the water" jokes aside, this file actually spooks me out every time I read it, especially since I keep finding new hidden texts. It's just great. +1 since I had forgotten to rate this

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While sitting in the warmth of my bedroom, this managed to make me feel cold. Hard +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I recognise them

item.title: (no title)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The trick you did with white text and selection was very clever, had me looking for more on the page.

item.title: thanks
item.content:encoded:
I remember this being the first SCP I ever heard. Beautifully written and the hidden details too.
Thank you for introducing me in this confusing world, you beautiful bastard +1

item.title: Re: Possible Typo?
item.content:encoded: Pretty sure it was intentional

item.title: Possible Typo?
item.content:encoded: Is it supposed to say staf at the end like a glitch or was that unintentional.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How do you find the collapsables on mobile

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ohhhhhhh.

This is good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok, this new css-thingy not revealing the 'blackbox' is kinda… unfortunate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The hallucinations might not be as literal as that. It could warp into something somebody can't recognize but still beam the idea of "recognize the bodies" into them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't remember if I said this but suffering from severe faceblindness, I literally do not recognize the bodies in the water. I mean they could all be copies of me and I STILL wouldn't recognize the bodies in the water. So easy solution for this SCP have all the gusrds be severely faceblind so none of them ever think they recognize the bodies in the water

item.title: Re: I think I'm starting to get some of the common complaints around here...
item.content:encoded:
While I think its completely fine to dislike the way this article uses collapsible and white text, I would say that it the usage of these elements is hinted at within the article. The sixth collapsible literally tells you to click below, the last verification test has an extra line that neither of the previous ones had, and the end of the article has a bunch of extra empty space. The mysterious and creepy feel of the article also adds to this.

Again, I'm not telling you to like this or implying that you are in any way stupid, but I do think that the article does a fine job nudging the reader towards exploring for stuff like hidden collapsibles and white text. Whether or not you like these elements or feel they serve the article well is completely up to you.

item.title: Re: I think I'm starting to get some of the common complaints around here...
item.content:encoded: Nah, I think it's pretty good the way it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, don't worry! They conveniently arrive just after your corpse has desiccated so that they won't have to waste resources. It's clean, time-saving, and efficient!

Join the SCP Foundation today!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i'm too distracted by the easter eggs and hidden things and format screw to pay attention to the story

item.title: I think I'm starting to get some of the common complaints around here...
item.content:encoded:
When I was in middle school, our class had been the trial class for a school-wide e-mail server, and we were able to send e-mails to conference groups between classes, as well as to each other. "All e-mail will be strictly monitored for inappropriate behavior," they threatened us, but in sixth grade, we thought we'd all be clever and put secret messages in white text that couldn't be read unless you were 'in the know' about it. We made secret messages and inside jokes, consisting of mostly harmless material, and we thought it was all the rage because everybody would put something in white text if they knew about it (i.e. 'everyone who's anyone'). But alas, the best laid plans of mice and 6th graders afted agley pretty quickly, because trying keeping a secret from teachers while also telling all of your classmates never works and we got busted when someone put "ur mom gay" in white text in their automatic e-mail signature or something equally stupid.

So white text can be something very cool, and I don't doubt that it's use can add to a skip for those 'in the know,' my largest gripe with this piece is two-fold. Firstly, is that not every skip has white text, and so I among the average readers would not check as automatically for these hidden bits of info as I would have in middle school. While this itself isn't problematic per se, my second gripe is that behind the white-text dropdowns, significantly more context for this skip is given. This is context that not only had me re-evaluate my understanding of this entry, but this re-evaluation came several months after my first reading. Had this context been more easily accessible, I would +1 without hesitation. After my first read-through, however, I no-voted.

I would be so much more inclined to leave my no-vote if those extra bits didn't have such a large impact on this skip's value, or even if the concept of white-text was only hinted at within the piece. Those footnotes were exceptional at building up this piece's backbone; couldn't they have hinted at Ctrl-A for help even once?

I only learned about these details while scrolling through /r/SCPDeclassified/top over the weekend on happenstance. As a result, I feel frustrated with this piece, but I still feel like those 'artfully concealed' dropdowns could be more effective with a few tweaks. -1 until those tweaks are made.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Awesome skip, I like it! I'm still waiting for medical staff though, how long is it going to take for them to get here?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do not recognize the bodies in the water.

...or do I?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ok

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found this article extremely annoying. After about 10 minutes of fruitlessly looking at the article for what I was missing, I was forced to open up the edit window to be able to read it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Essentially, the bodies in the water have altered the bottom of the article and try to convince anybody who reads it that they should go into the water. There's also a crazy long footnote (690 words 3,534 characters) in the hidden part that prevents the article from showing the footnotes at the bottom of the page. Don't worry, you didn't miss anything important.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice format screw. Kind of hard to find, but very interesting!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm really sorry for being that guy but I only read on mobile and i was wondering if someone could, spoilered, tell me what I'm missing

item.title: I recognize the bodies in the water
item.content:encoded: also 2521 black string boy

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Truly an entertaining read, my favorite Cognitohazard so far.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's why it's called a "format" screw.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's a hidden collapsible at the end, that's where the long footnote is

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: None of the footnotes are longer than a couple of lines, so I don't really see how that's "really long." Also, every other article with footnotes has them clearly displayed at the bottom, not showing them is a somewhat significant change to the established format.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The footnotes are intentionlly hidden as to not spoil anything, and also because one of the footnotes is really long.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's something wrong with the footnotes, they're showing up in the article but there's nothing at the bottom. They also skip from 6 to 8

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This feels like a very powerful and ominous phrase bloated into being a Full Meta Article for no compelling narrative reason. The narrative itself wasn't that interesting, and I sighed when I realized I needed to go to the page source to figure out what the article is Really Trying To Tell You Through The Dreadful Cracks And Untold Secrets Of The Foundation™.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This makes me think of the kelpie, or perhaps the aughisky.

Also, I knew perfectly well that 'I' was going to be corrupted by the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nah.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good idea I think, but the hidden and hiddener-things on the main page kill the pacing dead. If I have to go to the comments to find where the rest of the article is, I'm probably not going to go back and read it. -1, would be much better as a single integrated article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Short. Sweet. Creepy. Easily +1'd, because I am now reluctant to look out my bedroom window, where a bay is visible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Can't you hear them?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like others, I had to go through edit source to view the interview and other parts of it. Also like others, I have a hard time being convinced that I could ever be convinced I was a member of the class of 76, what with the way that would mean I was six years younger than my father. Even just cutting the bit where it tries to remind me of the summer of 1975 would do wonders for it, IMO. I still like it, but I don't like it as much as I did before it tried to convince me of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also the implication that something keeps modifying the documentation itself and/or giving researchers access they shouldn't have, so much so that there's an automated system blocking the edits. Could just be other people affected by the skip, could be the skip itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
WHY IS THERE NOT AN "UPVOTE COMMENT" BUTTON?!?!

+1 anyway. You can't tell me what to do

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably because the cognitohazard itself is hard to contain, and just seeing it will trigger the anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This got me. This got me good. Definite +1.

Is the only reason it is keter due to the fact there are possibly still more uncontained anomalous items which may draw people to it? It seems fairly secure behind a fence and armed guard…just curious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Plenty of people have enjoyed it just fine on mobile. I can open every collapsible and see every footnote on my note 8 with little issue.

That said, the wiki is best experienced on a full monitor with mouse support, and the mobile view shouldn't be relied on.

Edit: I'm also not certain you know what "ham fisted" means.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You should always use Options->View Page Source instead of Edit, that way there's no risk of accidentally editing the article and you aren't locking out other users.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This sounds like a good article. An intersting cognitohazard that aims for the viewer directly—a tad of a low hanging fruit in the metanarrative focused spooky tone the SCP Community has nowadays, yeah, but it’s got an ominous ring to it all the same with the tome of the article breaking to its advantage. That should be interesting!

Now if only I could read the article

I don’t recognize the complaint in this comment
Apologies for the snarky bit of quipping, not trying to be meanspirited or anything, just a cheeky attempt at relevance

Now, let me make this point a bit clear; I read, I browse, I do almost everything internet related off of my iPad, more specifically its default browser app Safari. And although it’s in my opinion a perfectly usable app, it and the device it uses does have its drawbacks. For example, the inability to meaningfully let me interact with the articles formatting style. There’s an interview? Well I’m looking at this page, I’m highlighting and clicking on everything, and let me say, no there isn’t. Neither are the little footers actually visible on the page! I’m just here, looking at an article that’s talking about bodies I recognize in the water, implying there’s more, and then it abruptly ends.

The only way I can view this article is by viewing it in a state that makes it apear as if it’s half-finished. And to read the whole thing, I have to scroll down and view the article through the edit function so I can see the whole thing. And to tell the truth, I’m not appreciative of that level of meta, because that’s when a story starts feeling almost hamfisted in getting a point through in my opinion.


There’s an interesting story and concept that’s nice and chilling, but the formatting of it all and the fact it’s core conceit is generally just following through on something now very common on this site which I find generally disinteresting (which although good this may be, is still that disinteresting thing to me) means I personally have to give it a downvote. It’s a good work, but not an accessible one and generally not my cup of tea, even if I’ll acknowledge it’s a nice one all the same.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, is that Kyle in there?

item.title: Re: Class of '75
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2316 hosts a powerful cognitohazardous effect, causing those who have viewed SCP-2316, are aware of certain parts of its nature, or who were enrolled in the [COGNITOHAZARD REMOVED] [Automated Message: INVALID] to believe that the individual instances of SCP-2316 are human beings they recognize, typically from their childhood.

I tend to take these things at face value. It doesn't mention exceptions, therefore the anomaly gives not a damn about when someone was born.

I've got a pretty good idea about how that would work, too...

item.title: Re: Class of '75
item.content:encoded:
If you want to be literal, this skip and other class of 76 ones should only work on people born in 1958, since 18 years after that they would have graduated and would be apart of the class of 76.

It's also tecnically the Class of '76 not the class of 75

item.title: Class of '75
item.content:encoded: I wonder if this SCP also affects people that have been born after 1975. After all, it would not make any sense for, say, a teenager to remember a class trip to a lake in 1975.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spooky scary waterbois are cognitohazardously forcing me to +1 this send help

/s if you needed it

Seriously tho this is good

E: I'm not that big of a fan of the new formatting. It just gets in the way for me, and the COGNITOHAZARD WARNING was a perfectly good way to begin the skip. I'm not going to take away my +1, but this does bother me.

item.title: So basically...
item.content:encoded: So basically the bodies in the water appear as the viewer's friends and family?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read everything in the SCP, including all the hidden stuff, but I'm still not sure as to what this is. It seems that there's a bunch of psychic corpses floating around in a lake and their sending me a bunch of subliminal messages to go and see them at the lake. I don't understand why though. What's so significant about the bodies, and why it's so important that I go back to them.

Also, what exactly is the class of '76?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At the least you'd know something was seriously wrong if you started to.
